PRODUCT BUILDER CASE STUDY

From Protocol to Product

Turning the Qualys MCP server into a Risk Copilot

qualys-cli-mcp16 tools · 12 modulesone governance layer
Mayukh · github.com/mayukhg/qualys-mcp-app
THE THESIS

A protocol is not a product.

A protocol is not a product.

pip install qualys-cli-mcp takes ninety seconds. Everything that makes it usable by a security team happens after.

CAPABILITY · 16 tools, 12 modules, one governance layer
THE PRODUCT WORK · this deck
OUTCOME · a running, governed, auditable risk copilot
WHY THIS MCP SERVER

What the server actually is

LLM

Claude, GPT, Llama — speaks MCP, holds no credentials

qualys-cli-mcp

Validates argv, redacts secrets, enforces policy, writes the audit log

qualys-cli

Auth, retries, the actual Qualys API calls

Qualys Cloud

VM · PC · PM · WAS · TotalCloud · CS · CSAM · Cloud Agent

The server never touches Qualys directly. It wraps an existing CLI and puts a governance layer in front of it — which is precisely why it can be productised rather than demoed.
WHY THIS MCP SERVER

Three unlocks

01

One thread, twelve modules

Natural language replaces hand-built QQL across VM, PC, PM, WAS, TotalCloud, Container Security, CSAM and Cloud Agent.

02

No API client to build

Sixteen typed tools plus a generic passthrough. The integration cost of a cross-module workflow collapses to a config entry.

03

Guardrails in the box

Argv validation, keyring credentials, module allowlist, read-only mode, append-only JSONL audit — shipped, not bolted on.

WHY THIS MCP SERVER

Capability was never the blocker

Security teams have not pointed LLMs at security data for three reasons. This server answers all three.

CREDENTIALS

“Who holds the keys?”

Secrets sit in the OS keyring. They are never passed to the model, and they are redacted out of tool output.

BLAST RADIUS

“What can it break?”

DENY_WRITE forces read-only. ALLOWED_MODULES bounds the session to the modules you name.

PROVENANCE

“Prove what it did.”

Every tool call lands in an append-only JSONL log with actor, module, mode and timestamp.

This is the part most MCP demos never touch — and the only part an enterprise buyer actually asks about.
THE SOLUTION SPACE

Eleven doors

Every use case the server supports. Listing them is the easy half.

Query & triage
  • Conversational VM triage
  • Policy Compliance posture Q&A
  • WAS findings summarisation
  • Cloud misconfiguration review
  • Container image risk gate
  • CSAM risk-ranking lookups
  • Cloud Agent coverage checks
Decision support
  • Patch prioritisation across VM + PM
Distribution
  • Copilot inside Claude Desktop / IDE
  • Governed read-only self-service reporting
Platform
  • Custom security tooling built on qualys_cli() and qualys_cli_help()
THE SOLUTION SPACE

How I cut eleven to one

Four criteria, stated before the scoring — not reverse-engineered from the answer.

CandidateNeeds a cross-module joinExercises the governance layerReplaces a recurring ritualHas a second audience
Conversational VM triage○○○○
Patch prioritisation●○○○
Container / cloud misconfig review○○○○
Governed self-service reporting○●○●
Unified Cross-Domain Risk Copilot●●●●
The winner is the only candidate that needs a join no single Qualys screen gives you, exercises the guardrails that are the actual moat, replaces a named ritual (the Monday leadership report), and ships with a second audience already in it.
DEEP DIVE

The problem, in the customer's words

01

“Nothing tells us what to fix first.”

Hosts, cloud resources, containers and web apps each rank separately. There is no single list.

02

“We join it by hand.”

An analyst opens six modules to answer one question about one asset. Every week.

03

“The Monday report is a spreadsheet.”

Defensible to leadership, provable to nobody. No record of what was pulled, or when.

04

“Engineering wants answers, not access.”

Managers need to know how risky their estate is — without a Qualys seat or write permissions.

DEEP DIVE

Requirements, mapped to tools

The decomposition is the design. Every business question resolves to a named tool and a named module.

Business needMCP toolModule
What are our riskiest assets right now?Asset risk-ranking / inventoryCSAM
What is wrong with them, and how bad is it?Detection + host queries + KB lookupVM
Is a failing control compounding the risk?Posture assessmentPC
Is it an internet-facing app with findings?Findings listWAS
Is it cloud-hosted and misconfigured?Connector + CDR dataTotalCloud
Does it run vulnerable container images?Image vulnerability scanCS
Is a fix already in flight?Job + patch listingsPM
Are we even watching this asset?Agent inventoryCloud Agent
Anything the curated tools missqualys_cli() + qualys_cli_help()Generic
Safe self-service, provable to auditorsDENY_WRITE + ALLOWED_MODULES + AUDIT_LOGGovernance
DEEP DIVE

The weekly workflow

1 · Identify

Rank the top assets — CSAM

2 · Explain

Turn CVE IDs into a narrative — VM + KB

3 · Compound

Flag controls that make it worse — PC

4 · Branch

Follow the asset type — WAS / TC / CS

5 · Blind spots

Name what we cannot see — CA

6 · Fix

In flight, or needs a job? — PM

7 · Gap-fill

The bespoke ask — qualys_cli()

8 · Report

One ranked list, one exec summary

Eight modules, one narrative. This is a join — not eight queries printed next to each other.
DEEP DIVE

The copilot reports what it cannot see.

The copilot reports what it cannot see.

Two of the five assets in the demo tenant have no Cloud Agent — an internet-facing VPN gateway and a production database in eu-west-1. Every risk score on them is an underestimate. The copilot says so out loud instead of ranking them politely low.

Most tools report findings
This one reports the absence of findings
A coverage gap is a finding, not a blank row
DEEP DIVE

Governance is the packaging, not the fine print

Analyst

the Monday risk sweep
ModulesAll 8 in scope
ModeRead-only (DENY_WRITE=1)
EvidenceFull cross-module chain
AuditEvery call, JSONL
same
copilot

Manager · self-service

“how risky is my team's estate?”
ModulesCSAM, VM, WAS only
ModeRead-only (DENY_WRITE=1)
EvidenceRestricted modules hidden and labelled
AuditEvery call, JSONL
Same copilot. Two products. The difference is two environment variables — and, as the next slide shows, the server actually enforces them.
PROOF

Same app. Same backend. Two rails.

Analyst

CSAM VM PC WAS TC CS PM CA
  1. 1
    Stay on the default profile no call · 8/8 modules, read-only
  2. 2
    Ask for the ranked risk list POST /api/chat → csam.risk_ranking
  3. 3
    Scan the ranking prod-checkout-web-01 · 96 · CRITICAL
  4. 4
    Expand the top asset GET /api/assets/…/evidence · 5/5 visible
  5. 5
    Ask a cross-module follow-up POST /api/chat → module CA
  6. 6
    Try a write command anyway POST /api/qualys-cli → 403 blocked
  7. 7
    Open the audit log GET /api/audit
Leaves with a cross-module remediation picture — and a defensible trail of how it was built.

Manager · self-service

CSAM VM WAS · 5 locked
  1. 1
    Switch profiles POST /api/session {profile:"manager"}
  2. 2
    Workbench refreshes same scores — CSAM ranking stays open
  3. 3
    Expand the same asset GET /api/assets/…/evidence · 2/5 visible
  4. 4
    Ask why it's risky POST /api/chat → module VM
  5. 5
    Ask about patches anyway POST /api/chat → module PM denied
  6. 6
    Open the audit log GET /api/audit · CSAM / VM / WAS only
Leaves with enough visibility to self-serve — and a paper trail proving the boundary held.
The ranking is never restricted — only the supporting detail underneath it. A manager can see that an asset scores 96; they cannot see the compliance failure or the patch job behind it. That was a decision, not a limitation.
PROOF

The guardrail, executed

$ curl -X POST localhost:5050/api/qualys-cli \
-d '{"command":"pm patch apply --job Q3-log4j-remediation"}'
HTTP/1.1 403 Forbidden
{
"output": "blocked: 'pm patch apply ...' looks like a
write operation and QUALYS_MCP_DENY_WRITE=1 is
enforced for this session."
}
# and in the audit log, one line later:
{"ts":"2026-07-14T07:17:18Z", "tool":"qualys_cli",
"module":"GENERIC", "mode":"read-only",
"result":"denied"}
I tried to make it do the wrong thing. The server said no — and then wrote down that I asked.
  • The model did not refuse. The server refused. That is the difference between a control and a hope.
  • DENY_WRITE applies to every profile, including the Analyst's — there is no privileged path around it.
  • The denial is audited, not just blocked. An auditor can see what was attempted, not only what succeeded.
DEEP DIVE

What changes

TodayWith the copilot
Ranking across domainsSix modules, joined by handOne ranked list
Time to the Monday reportHours of spreadsheet workOne conversation
ProvenanceNoneTimestamped JSONL, per tool call
Coverage gapsInvisibleNamed as a first-class finding
AudienceAnalysts with Qualys seatsPlus engineering managers, read-only
Directional. No live tenant data was used — these are the claims the prototype exists to test, not measured results.
THE SURFACE

Three principles I designed to

Each one is provable in the screenshots that follow.

01

Never assert. Cite.

Every claim carries the module that produced it. There are no naked answers anywhere in the interface.

02

Governance in the chrome.

Read-only, the module allowlist and the audit count live in the header — not in a config file nobody opens.

03

One surface, two personas.

The same screen, re-scoped. Restriction is a designed state, not an error state.

THE SURFACE

The whole product in one frame

Conversation on the left. Ranked evidence on the right. The governance contract across the top.
  • The header states the contract before you ask anything: read-only, 8 of 8 modules in scope, and a live audit counter.
  • This is a running app — a Node server enforcing the policy, not a styled mockup.
THE SURFACE

The conversation carries its receipts

Every answer is stamped with the tools that produced it.
  • “Show top risk assets org-wide” resolves to csam.risk_ranking.
  • “Why is prod-checkout-web-01 risky?” resolves to vm.detections · pc.posture.
  • “Any assets missing agent coverage?” resolves to ca.inventory.
  • An analyst can defend the answer without trusting the model.
THE SURFACE

One asset. Five modules. One story.

Expand prod-checkout-web-01 (TruRisk 96) and all five evidence cards render — 5/5 visible, because every module they live in is inside the Analyst allowlist.
  • VM — Log4Shell, CVSS 10.0, KB confirms active exploitation.
  • WAS — reflected SQL injection on /checkout/apply-promo.
  • PC — fails CIS 5.2.9, SSH root login is enabled.
  • CA — agent healthy, so this score can be trusted.
  • PM — a remediation job exists and has not run. Expedite it.
THE SURFACE

The audit log proves the negative

Append-only JSONL: timestamp, tool, module, actor, mode, result. Written for every call — including the ones that were denied.
  • Most audit logs record what happened. This one lets you prove what didn't.
  • The Manager's trail is visibly shorter than the Analyst's — evidence that the session never touched PC, TC, CS, PM or CA, without taking anyone's word for it.
  • That is the difference between compliance theatre and a control.
THE SURFACE

The same product, re-scoped

Manager profile, same asset, same endpoint: 2 of 5 evidence cards render. The server decides, not the stylesheet.
  • The banner names exactly what hid: “3 modules hidden under the manager allowlist (PC, CA, PM).” Nothing is silently dropped.
  • Ask about patches anyway and the copilot says PM is out of scope — the restriction surfaces in the conversation, not in a log the manager will never read.
  • Restriction is a designed state, not an error state.
HOW I BUILT IT

The loop

01

Read the spec — all 16 tools, all 12 modules

02

Map tools to business needs, not to features

03

Cut eleven to one, on criteria stated first

04

Design the workflow as a join, not a query list

05

Build the surface until the guardrails actually run

06

Pressure-test the guardrails as a persona, not a setting

Honest scope: this is a running application — a Node server that enforces the allowlist, rejects writes, and writes its own audit log. What it does not have is a live Qualys tenant behind it. Production needs a real MCP session against real data, a latency and cost budget, and an eval harness for hallucination on risk claims. Naming the gap accurately is part of the job.
WHAT'S NEXT

The server was the raw material. The product was the judgment.

v1 — the Monday ritualv2 — scheduled run, delivered to Slackv3 — write path with approval gatingv4 — an ETM consumption channel

Eleven doors. One opened. Made safe enough to walk through.
